human.en:
The Orange Line platform at Foggy Bottom fills at 8:47 every morning. The same bodies in the same coats, standing at the same painted stripe on the concrete. Nobody makes eye contact on the Metro. That is its own kind of intimacy — the practiced distance of people who share a city without sharing a life.

On weekends the rhythm changes. Eastern Market on a Saturday: coffee in a paper cup, the vendor who remembers how you take it, the smell of cut flowers and old paperbacks from the table outside. The Hill has a texture to it, red brick and elm canopy, the sound of screen doors, the particular quiet of a neighborhood that has been inhabited long enough to have habits.

Summer comes like something you forgot was possible. The air off the Potomac sits heavy over everything. People move slower. The evenings on porches, the corner store with the door propped open, the block where everyone knows the dog's name but not always the owner's — this is the city beneath the city of monuments and motorcades.

liminal.en:
Union Station at 6 a.m., before the coffee shops open. The great hall carries its own echo — footsteps that belong to no one in particular, announcements dissolving into the vaulted ceiling. Everyone here is between places. The city hasn't started yet; wherever they're going hasn't either.

The checkpoints on Pennsylvania Avenue where the street ends and the security perimeter begins. The bollards sunk into asphalt, the jersey barriers, the long view toward the Capitol that you are permitted to look at but not simply walk toward anymore. A city of thresholds: what is open and what is closed, what requires a badge and what does not, the credentialed and the uncredentialed passing through the same morning air.

The bridges over the Potomac at dusk. Virginia on one side, the District on the other, and the river moving between jurisdictions the way water does — without permission, without documentation. Standing on the Key Bridge, the water is neither place. The rush hour traffic vibrates the cables. You are technically crossing but for a moment you are simply suspended above the current.

environment.en:
The cherry blossoms come for about two weeks in late March and then the rain takes them. The Tidal Basin fills with petals floating on the surface, pale pink against the brown water. Then they are gone and the trees are just trees again for another year. The city organizes its calendar around this.

Rock Creek runs through the city from Maryland to the Potomac, carving a forested corridor that shouldn't exist in a dense urban grid but does. The creek floods in heavy rains, closes the parkway, deposits silt on the path where cyclists go in dry weather. The watershed remembers every storm. The bedrock here is Piedmont schist, folded and refolded over hundreds of millions of years, now holding up embassies and row houses.

August in the District is the city at its most honest. The humidity comes off the Chesapeake and sits. The marble of the monuments sweats. The tourists thin. The people who actually live here learn where the shade falls in the afternoon, which streets tunnel a breeze, how to read the sky over Virginia for the late-day thunderstorm moving northeast.

digital.en:
Thirty miles west on the Dulles corridor, in Ashburn and Sterling, the data centers stand in long rows behind chain-link and camera arrays. More internet traffic passes through Loudoun County than anywhere else on earth. The buildings are unremarkable — beige, windowless, surrounded by parking and backup generators — but inside, the racks hum at a frequency that is nearly inaudible and continuous.

The federal agencies run their own networks that run alongside and beneath the civilian internet. Classified and unclassified, air-gapped and connected, the signal infrastructure of governance spreads outward from agency campuses in Bethesda and Langley and Fort Meade. Every cleared contractor, every lobbyist with a secure device, every Senate staffer emailing a draft — it all moves through fiber buried under the Beltway.

In the coffee shops on H Street, the faces angle toward screens. Everyone here seems to be monitoring something — a bill, a hearing, a thread, a vote count. The city runs on information the way other cities run on manufacturing. The raw material is attention; the product is influence; the infrastructure is invisible.

infrastructure.en:
The Metro opened in 1976 and some of the original cars are still running. The system was designed for a different ridership pattern, a different city. The deferred maintenance accumulated for decades — signals, power cables, the concrete vault panels that began falling. SafeTrack, the 2016 shutdown program, closed segments for weeks at a time to do what should have been done continuously. The city reorganized itself around the gaps.

The water comes from the Potomac, treated at the Washington Aqueduct, pushed through pipe networks that date in places to the Civil War. Lead service lines still run under some neighborhoods, a problem that has been known and documented and partially addressed and is still being addressed. The infrastructure of any city is also a record of which problems were fixed when and for whom.

I-495 encircles the city like a boundary condition. The traffic models show it saturating at rush hour, have always shown this, will continue to show this regardless of lane additions. The interchange at Springfield is known as the Mixing Bowl. Seventeen lanes of classified interstate, elevated and crossing and merging — a structure so complex it has become a kind of landmark, the region's infrastructure made monumental.

more_than_human.en:
The Atlantic flyway runs through here. In October and March, the sky over the Chesapeake marshes moves with birds — Canada geese, tundra swans, canvasbacks, the smaller shorebirds in flocks that shift direction simultaneously without apparent signal. They have been following this route for longer than the city has existed. The city is an interruption in a path that predates it.

White-tailed deer browse in Rock Creek Park and cross River Road at dusk, moving between the park corridor and the suburban lots where there are still enough trees to provide cover. The coyotes arrived in the District around 2004, dispersed from the Appalachians down through the piedmont. They are there in the park and along the creek corridors, mostly unseen, having found the interstices of the city that human attention passes over.

The Anacostia River is recovering. For decades it was among the most polluted urban rivers in the country — combined sewer overflows, stormwater runoff, decades of industrial use along its banks. The river is still impaired but there are more species now. River otters were documented again in 2019. The water is doing something the policy documents call restoration but which is really more like the river reasserting itself in the gaps left by reduced pressure.
